entry.title: Linear Algebra Notes
entry.content:
Linear Algebra

Introduction

  • 矩阵的基本性质与线性方程组 (§6, §7, §8)
  • 行列式 (§9, §26)
  • 线性无关 (§11)
  • 子空间 (§27)

Basic Properties of Matrix

Definition: Special kinds of matrices

  • Square matrix: $m = n$
  • Diagonal matrix: $m = n$ and $\alpha_{ij} = 0$ for $i \neq j$
  • Identity matrix $I$: diagonal matrix with $\alpha_{ii} = 1$ for all $i$
  • Upper-triangular: $\alpha_{ij} = 0$ for $i > j$
  • Lower-triangular: $\alpha_{ij} = 0$ for $i < j$
  • Symmetric: $\alpha{ij} = \alpha{ji}$ for all $i, j$
  • Idempotent: A square matrix $A$ is called an idempotent matrix if $AA = A$

Definition: 转置和逆

Transpose: The $n \times m$ matrix $A’$ is the transpose of the $m \times n$ matrix $A$ if $a’{ji} = a{ij}$.

Properties:

  1. $(A’)’ = A$
  2. $(A + B)’ = A’ + B’$
  3. $(AB)’ = B’A’$

Inverse: The inverse of matrix $A$, $A^{-1}$, is defined only if $A$ is a square matrix, and $AA^{-1} = A^{-1}A = I$.

A square matrix may or may not have an inverse.
If a square matrix does have an inverse, it is called a nonsingular matrix; otherwise, it is called a singular matrix.

Properties:

  1. If an inverse exists, it is unique.
  2. $( A^{-1} )^{-1} = A$
  3. $( AB )^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}$
  4. $( A’ )^{-1} = ( A^{-1} )’$

Rank and Determinant

Definition: Elementary row operations

Elementary row operations:

  1. Interchange two rows of a matrix
  2. Change a row by adding to it a multiple of another row
  3. Multiply each element in a row by the same nonzero number

The elementary row operations can be performed on a matrix $A$ by premultiplying $A$ by certain special matrices called elementary matrices. The matrices $E{ij}$, $E{ij}(r)$, and $E_i(r)$, which are obtained by performing elementary row operations on the identity matrix, are called elementary matrices.

Definition: Row echelon form

A row of a matrix has $k$ leading zeros if the first $k$ elements of the row are all zeros and the $(k+1)^{\text{th}}$ element of the row is not zero. A matrix is in row echelon form if each row has more leading zeros than the row preceding it.

For example,

Definition: Pivot and Rank

A pivot of a matrix in row echelon form is an element which is the first nonzero element in its row.

A row echelon matrix in which each pivot is a 1 and in which each column containing a pivot contains no other nonzero entries is said to be in reduced row echelon form.

For example,

The rank of a matrix $A$, denoted $\operatorname{rank}(A)$ or $r(A)$, is the number of nonzero rows in any of its row echelon forms (equivalent to the number of pivots).

Proposition: Properties of determinant

  1. For any $n \times n$ matrix $A$,
    $\det A = \det A’$

  2. The interchange of any two rows will only alter the sign.

  3. If two rows (or columns) of $A$ are equal, then
    $\det A = 0$

  4. Let $A$ and $B$ be $n \times n$ matrices which differ only in their $i$-th row. Let $C$ be the matrix whose $i$-th row is the sum of the $i$-th rows of $A$ and $B$, and whose other rows are identical to those of $A$ and $B$. Then:

  5. Multiplying a row by a scalar $k$ scales the determinant by $k$:

  6. Adding a multiple of one row to another row leaves the determinant unchanged:

  7. Expanding a determinant by alien cofactors (cofactors of a different row/column) always yields zero:

    where $C_{jk}$ are the cofactors of the $j$-th row.
    （相当于构造了一个行列式中有两行相等的新矩阵）

Lemma: Elementary matrix and row echelon form

  1. For any $k \times n$ matrix $A$, there exist elementary matrices $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_m$ such that

    where $U$ is in (reduced) row echelon form.

  2. Any matrix $A$ can be written as

    where the $F_i$’s are elementary matrices and $U$ is in reduced row echelon form. If $A$ is nonsingular, then $U = I$.

  3. Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix and let $R$ be its row echelon form. Then

    If no row interchanges are used to compute $R$ from $A$, then

  4. Let $E$ be an $n \times n$ elementary matrix and let $B$ be an arbitrary $n \times n$ matrix. Then,

Proposition

For arbitrary $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$,

Matrix and Linear Systems of Equations

The analysis of an economic model often reduces to the study of a system of linear equations:

We can write the system as a matrix equation:

where $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix, $x$ is an $n$-vector, and $b$ is an $m$-vector.

It is important to determine:

  1. Whether the linear system $Ax = b$ has a solution
  2. How large the solution set is

Example

The coefficient matrix $A$, the right-hand side vector $b$, and the variable vector $x$ are:

We write the system compactly as:

The augmented matrix is:

Using Cramer’s Rule, each variable $x_i$ is given by

where $A_i$ is the matrix $A$ with its $i$-th column replaced by the vector $b$.

First, calculate $\det(A)$:

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub.

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment
